text: I've been sitting for about 6 months now, usually 20 minutes in the morning. Lately I've noticed my mind wanders constantly to planning my day. Should I just return to the breath each time or is there a technique to prevent the planning thoughts from arising in the first place?

text: Can anyone explain the difference between samatha and vipassana meditation? I've been doing breath awareness for a year and my teacher mentioned I should start incorporating vipassana but I'm not sure what changes in practice.

text: I tried meditating lying down for the first time because of back pain and immediately fell asleep. Is seated meditation essential or can lying down work if I'm disciplined enough to stay awake?

text: I've been reading about the two truths doctrine — conventional and ultimate truth. My question is whether the conventional truth is considered real in any sense or whether it's purely a pragmatic fiction we use to navigate experience.

text: The doctrine of anatta (non-self) seems to contradict the idea of karma and rebirth. If there is no self, what continues from one life to the next? How do Buddhist philosophers resolve this apparent contradiction?

text: I'm trying to understand the difference between Theravada and Mahayana conceptions of the bodhisattva ideal. In Theravada, is the goal purely individual liberation or is there also an element of compassionate service to others?

text: I've read that the Buddha refused to answer certain metaphysical questions — like whether the world is eternal, whether the self persists after death. Why did he refuse? Was this pragmatic or did he have a philosophical position on why these questions can't be answered?

text: Can someone explain the concept of Buddha-nature as taught in the Tathagatagarbha sutras? Is Buddha-nature compatible with the doctrine of anatta or does it smuggle in a kind of self through the back door?

text: What is the Buddhist philosophical position on free will? If all phenomena arise from causes and conditions and there is no self, is there any room for genuine agency or choice?

text: I'm struggling to understand the Zen concept of 'original face' or 'original nature.' How does this concept relate to the mainstream Buddhist doctrine of no-self? Are they compatible?

text: My practice has been inconsistent this year due to health issues. I wanted to say publicly that returning to sitting after weeks away is always gentler than I expect. The practice doesn't judge the absence.
label: personal

text: For anyone interested in Theravada practice, I can't recommend Bhikkhu Bodhi's translations highly enough. His translation of the Majjhima Nikaya with commentary is the most comprehensive English resource for serious study of the Pali canon.
label: resource

text: The Insight Meditation Society (IMS) in Barre, Massachusetts runs excellent retreats for all levels. They have a scholarship program for those who can't afford full price. Their website is dharma.org.
label: resource

text: If you're looking for a good introduction to Zen, I'd suggest starting with Shunryu Suzuki's Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind rather than jumping straight into the koans. It's short, accessible, and genuine.
label: resource

text: For those interested in Tibetan Buddhism, the Rigpa organization has centers worldwide and offers both in-person and online teachings. Their free online programs are a good starting point before committing to a center.
label: resource

text: Ajahn Chah's collected teachings are available free at forestsangha.org. His book 'A Still Forest Pool' is probably the best single introduction to Thai forest tradition practice I've found.
label: resource

text: The Waking Up app by Sam Harris has its limitations but for complete beginners who want guided meditation without a specifically Buddhist framework, it's one of the better starting points. First month is free.
label: resource

text: If you want to study the Abhidharma, Nina van Gorkom's free books at zolag.co.uk are excellent. She explains the concepts clearly without oversimplifying them.
label: resource

text: Tricycle magazine has a large archive of free articles at tricycle.org and a paid subscription gives access to their online courses, which are taught by serious teachers. Worth it if you want structured study.
label: resource

text: For Mahayana philosophy, Jay Garfield's translation of Nagarjuna's Mulamadhyamakakarika is the best scholarly English edition. His philosophical commentary is essential — don't read the text without it.
label: resource
notes: "Could be philosophy but primary value is book recommendation"

text: The Audio Dharma website at audiodharma.org has thousands of free dharma talks from Gil Fronsdal and many other Insight Meditation teachers. All free, no signup required. An incredible resource.
label: resource

text: If you're in the New York area, the Village Zendo offers open sits and is genuinely welcoming to beginners. Sensei Egyoku Nakao teaches there periodically. No cost to attend open sits.
label: resource

text: For beginners who want a book-length introduction to Buddhism without going into a specific tradition, 'What the Buddha Taught' by Walpola Rahula is the classic. Short, clear, Theravada-oriented.
label: resource

text: The Dharma Seed podcast (dharmaseed.org) archives thousands of retreat talks for free. If you want to hear what teachers actually say in extended teaching contexts rather than just YouTube clips, this is the place.
label: resource

text: If you're interested in Tibetan Buddhism specifically, Chögyam Trungpa's 'Cutting Through Spiritual Materialism' is essential reading regardless of tradition. The concept of spiritual materialism applies universally.
label: resource

text: For a rigorous academic introduction to Buddhist philosophy, Mark Siderits's 'Buddhism as Philosophy' is excellent. It treats Buddhist positions seriously as philosophical claims and compares them to Western analogues.
label: resource

text: The Plum Village app is free and has a large library of guided meditations in Thich Nhat Hanh's tradition. Particularly good for beginners or for those who want mindfulness practice that isn't heavily soteriological.
label: resource

text: If you want to study with a traditional Tibetan teacher online, Lama Zopa Rinpoche's FPMT organization has extensive free online resources including entire lam-rim courses at fpmt.org.
label: resource

text: Gil Fronsdal's podcast series 'Introduction to Meditation' available on Audio Dharma is the best structured beginning I've found — 10 episodes that build systematically from basic breath awareness to more advanced practices.
label: resource

text: For anyone interested in the relationship between Buddhism and science, B. Alan Wallace's 'The Attention Revolution' is a good starting point. More rigorous than most popular books on the topic.
label: resource

text: The Zen Mountain Monastery in New York State (zmm.org) runs weekend and week-long introductory retreats that are genuinely accessible to people with no prior experience. Application process is straightforward.
label: resource

text: If you're interested in the history of Buddhism rather than just the practice, Donald Lopez's 'The Story of Buddhism' is an excellent overview that covers all major traditions with academic rigor.
label: resource

text: For those who prefer to learn by listening, Tara Brach's podcast is consistently excellent and freely available. She's particularly good on the intersection of Western psychology and Buddhist practice.
label: resource

text: If you want to understand Pure Land Buddhism, Taitetsu Unno's 'River of Fire, River of Water' is the best introduction in English. Accessible without being dumbed down.
label: resource

text: The Center for Mindfulness at UMass Medical School was founded by Jon Kabat-Zinn and offers MBSR programs online and in person. Not explicitly Buddhist but the most evidence-based mindfulness training available.
label: resource

text: For Theravada ordination or extended study in the Forest tradition, Abhayagiri Monastery in California (abhayagiri.org) offers long-term residency programs and publishes free books by Ajahn Pasanno and others.
label: resource

text: Bhikkhu Analayo's books on meditation are the most rigorous scholarly-practitioner resources I've found. His 'Satipatthana: The Direct Path to Realization' is the definitive study of the Satipatthana Sutta.
label: resource

text: If you're a beginner looking for a guided retreat experience without the intensity of a full silent retreat, Spirit Rock Meditation Center in California runs weekend programs specifically for people new to meditation.
label: resource

text: The Pali Text Society (palitext.com) has published the entire Pali canon in English. Expensive to buy everything but much of it is available through university libraries or free on Access to Insight at accesstoinsight.org.
label: resource

text: For a good contemporary introduction to Buddhist ethics, Damien Keown's 'Buddhist Ethics: A Very Short Introduction' covers the major issues concisely and fairly across traditions.
label: resource

text: The Everyday Dharma podcast by Sensei Koshin Paley Ellison is excellent for those interested in Zen and its application to everyday life, including work, relationships, and caregiving.
label: resource

text: If you're interested in early Buddhism and the historical Buddha, Johannes Bronkhorst's scholarship is the most rigorous available, though it's academic reading. His 'Buddhist Teaching in India' is a starting point.
label: resource

text: For anyone who finds books difficult due to attention or reading issues, the YouTube channel Buddhist Society of Western Australia posts talks by Ajahn Brahm with over 400 free teachings available.
label: resource

text: The app Insight Timer has thousands of free guided meditations from teachers across traditions. It also has a timer for unguided practice and a community forum. Free tier is genuinely excellent.
label: resource

text: If you want to understand how Zen came to the West, 'Shoes Outside the Door' by Michael Downing is the most honest account. It covers the San Francisco Zen Center and Suzuki Roshi's successors.
label: resource

text: The Barre Center for Buddhist Studies (bcbsdhamma.org) offers online courses that go deeper than most popular dharma programs. Their 'Foundations of Dharma' program is one of the best structured curricula I've found.
label: resource

text: For anyone interested in socially engaged Buddhism, Thich Nhat Hanh's 'Interbeing' is the founding text of the movement. Short and accessible.
label: resource

text: The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy has excellent free entries on Buddhist philosophy — on topics like Buddhist ethics, the metaphysics of personal identity, and Madhyamaka. Academic but clear.
label: resource

text: If you're in the UK, the Buddhist Society in London (thebuddhistsociety.org) has a good library and runs regular talks. Their journal The Middle Way has been published since 1926 and archives are online.
label: resource

text: For those who want to go deeper into Vajrayana, Sogyal Rinpoche's 'The Tibetan Book of Living and Dying' remains the most accessible introduction to the tradition for Western readers.
label: resource

text: The Zen Peacemakers organization founded by Bernie Glassman does street retreats and social action programs grounded in Zen practice. Their website zenpeacemakers.org has resources on engaged practice.
label: resource

text: If you're interested in how the early texts were formed, Bhikkhu Sujato and Bhikkhu Brahmali's work on suttacentral.net includes both translations and scholarly essays on the authenticity of early Buddhist texts. All free.
label: resource

text: For children's resources, Thich Nhat Hanh wrote a series of books designed to introduce Buddhist concepts to young readers. 'A Pebble for Your Pocket' is the most accessible starting point.
label: resource

text: The Empty Mirror by Janwillem van de Wetering is a memoir of practicing at a Japanese Zen monastery. Not a practice manual but an honest account of what the experience actually feels like.
label: resource

text: For academic work on meditation from a neuroscience perspective, Richard Davidson and Sharon Begley's 'The Emotional Life of Your Brain' is evidence-based without being reductive about the practice.
label: resource

text: If you want to understand the differences between Theravada traditions — Sri Lankan, Thai, Burmese — Bhikkhu Bodhi's articles on Access to Insight are the clearest comparative overview I've found.
label: resource

text: The Kripalu Center in Massachusetts offers weekend programs that introduce Buddhist and yogic practices together. Good option for people who aren't sure which tradition resonates with them.
label: resource

text: For anyone interested in practicing in a Tibetan center, the Shambhala network has centers in most major cities and offers structured levels of study. Their introductory programs are genuinely welcoming to beginners.
label: resource

text: Guo Gu's 'Passing Through the Gateless Barrier' is the best contemporary English-language guide to koan practice I've found. He teaches in both Chinese Chan and Japanese Zen lineages.
label: resource

text: The Shinzen Young network at shinzen.org offers online programs in his systematic mindfulness approach. His book 'The Science of Enlightenment' explains the system clearly.
label: resource

text: If you want a good community for online practice, the Dharma Overground forum (dharmaoverground.org) has experienced practitioners and honest discussion of practice stages that you won't find in most dharma communities.
label: resource
